content.problemStatement:
**This problem was part of the CodeChef April Challenge.  All user submissions for this contest problem are publicly available here.**

Bugs have gotten into The Chef’s kitchen!  Help him trap them all and he’ll make you a batch of his famous chocolate chip cookies.  You are given a given a square map of the kitchen divided into tiles, and in each tile sits some known number of bugs.  You also have a square trap, which can be dropped to cover a certain number of tiles (the trap may only cover tiles from within the map, and must be aligned to the borders of the kitchen). However, the trap only catches bugs from one of the tiles which it has covered, having a minimum number of bugs on it. For all possible positions at which the trap can be dropped, determine number of bugs that will be caught.
constraints: The number of bugs on each tile is a 32-bit signed integer
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers, T and n, where T is the number of test cases and n is the size of the map. The next line contains a space-separated integer, k, which is the size of the trap. The next n lines contain n space-separated integers each, representing the number of bugs on each tile.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of bugs that will be caught for each possible position of the trap, with each row representing a possible position of the trap.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each possible position of the trap, find the minimum number of bugs within the trap.
For each possible position of the trap, find the minimum number of bugs within the trap.
For each possible position of the trap, find the minimum number of bugs within the trap.

solution.approach: The key insight is to use a sliding window approach to find the minimum number of bugs within the trap for each possible position. We iterate over each possible position of the trap, and for each position, we find the minimum number of bugs within the trap by iterating over each tile covered by the trap.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2 * k^2) per test case, O(T * n^2 * k^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) — the dominant structure is the map.
